title_of_original: Case C-279/08 P - Judgment of the Court (Third Chamber) of 8 September 2011. European Commission v Kingdom of the Netherlands.
decided_by: Court of Justice of the European Union
case_description: [1] Directive (EU) 2020/1743 on national water sustainability ceilings requires Member States to take action in response to mounting pressure on groundwater reserves. [2] While the Directive sets binding targets, it leaves the choice of specific measures to the discretion of each Member State. [3] In this context, the Romanian government introduced a regulatory scheme to limit industrial freshwater extraction from overexploited aquifers. [4] The measure aimed to safeguard long-term environmental interests while encouraging more efficient water use by major industrial consumers. [5] The government set a national cap of 110 million cubic metres per year for industrial freshwater extraction from designated groundwater sources, [6] with the goal of meeting this limit by 2025. [7] To implement the cap, Romania established a system of freshwater extraction certificates. [8] These certificates were issued only to companies with installations with a daily pumping capacity above 15,000 cubic metres, [9] which meant that participation was restricted to large-scale users. [10] For each eligible installation the companies received an annual extraction ceiling. [11] This ceiling was determined by applying a fixed water-efficiency standard to the installation’s historical energy consumption. [12] At the start of each year, the government granted certificates reflecting this ceiling at no cost to the companies. [13] An expert committee tasked with reviewing the scheme concluded that it could reduce water extraction. [14] They also found that the certificates could have been sold without impairing the environmental goal. [15] In the committee’s view, selling the certificates would have produced the same effect on water consumption while also generating income to the state budget. [16] Undertakings were not required to hold certificates in advance in order to operate. [17] They were allowed to extract water throughout the year, provided that their total use by year’s end remained within the assigned limit. [18] If a facility extracted less than its ceiling, it could sell the surplus certificates to others who needed more secondary market. [19] If it exceeded the ceiling, it was required to buy additional certificates from other facilities to cover the excess, [20] and the shortfall was subject to a 25 percent surcharge. [21] Repeated non-compliance would lead to administrative fines. [22] The scheme applied only to large industrial operators. [23] Other substantial water users, such as agricultural producers and medium-sized manufacturers, were excluded from the certificate system. [24] They received neither extraction caps nor access to certificates. [25] The Romanian authorities justified this restriction by referring to environmental impact and practical concerns. [26] According to the government, large installations accounted for a disproportionate share of industrial water use and were therefore the most suitable targets for regulation.
